data-engineer.overview: This system implements a Python-based ML workflow for Rossmann-style retail sales forecasting ⚠️. Core components include data preprocessing and feature cube construction for model training and evaluation (⚠️ $.devInsight.codeOverview.components). Execution is organized around Jupyter notebooks for preprocessing, modeling, and evaluation (⚠️ $.devInsight.codeOverview.entrypoints). The primary implementation languages are Python and Jupyter Notebook ($.devInsight.codeOverview.languages). The package exposes handler-style public APIs for model fitting, prediction, and evaluation (⚠️ $.devInsight.architecture.publicApis). Test infrastructure is present and configured to run with coverage against the bhm_at_scale package (⚠️ $.devInsight.qualitySignals.testsPresent, ⚠️ $.devInsight.qualitySign

data-scientist.overview: The system focuses on forecasting daily sales for many retail stores using a Bayesian hierarchical model ($.business.useCase). The repository likely serves as a reproducible, educational example rather than a production deployment ⚠️ ($.business.intendedUse). It appears optimized for offline/batch analysis rather than real-time serving ⚠️ ($.business.outOfScopeUse). Primary users are expected to be data scientists and statisticians exploring scalable Bayesian modeling techniques ⚠️ ($.business.userPopulations). Safety- or regulation-critical decision-making is discouraged ⚠️ ($.business.hazardousUseCases).
data-scientist.changes:
- Clarified high-level purpose and scope of the project in the executive summary ⚠️ ($.business.executiveSummary).
- Specified the core sales forecasting use case with a Bayesian hierarchical model ($.business.useCase).
- Documented intended educational/research-oriented use, distinguishing it from production services ⚠️ ($.business.intendedUse, $.business.nonGoals).
- Tightened boundaries on out-of-scope real-time or low-latency usage ⚠️ ($.business.outOfScopeUse).
- Added guidance on hazardous use cases (e.g., safety-critical or regulated decisions) ⚠️ ($.business.hazardousUseCases).
- Further characterized target user populations as technical practitioners ⚠️ ($.business.userPopulations).

domain-expert.overview: This system implements a Bayesian hierarchical model for forecasting daily sales across many retail stores (see $.business.useCase). It is primarily aimed at data-rich, multi-entity time series where partial pooling and uncertainty quantification are important. ⚠️ The repository is likely positioned as an educational or research-focused implementation rather than a production service (see $.business.intendedUse, $.business.nonGoals). ⚠️ Expected users are domain experts, data scientists, and statisticians comfortable with Bayesian modeling workflows (see $.business.userPopulations).
domain-expert.changes:

- Intended use and non-goals clarified, emphasizing non-production, exploratory usage patterns (see $.business.intendedUse, $.business.nonGoals). ⚠️

governance-compliance-ethics-officer.overview: The ML system’s governance framework includes explicit licensing and policy information under $.governance.policies. The source code is licensed under GPL-2.0-or-later (GNU General Public License), which requires that derivative works and redistributed modifications remain under a compatible copyleft license. This has implications for how the system can be integrated into larger products, combined with proprietary components, and redistributed, and it should be reflected in compliance procedures, vendor assessments, and contractual terms with downstream users and partners.
governance-compliance-ethics-officer.changes:
- $.governance.policies now explicitly records that the source code is licensed under GPL-2.0-or-later (GNU General Public License).
- This clarification formalizes copyleft obligations for any redistribution or modification of the ML system.
- Compliance processes may need review to ensure downstream distribution, integration, and contribution workflows conform to GPL-2.0-or-later terms.
- Contracting and procurement teams should verify that third-party use, sublicensing, and integration scenarios remain compatible with this license designation and associated governance expectations documented in the system card.

governance-officer.overview: The ML system’s governance framework includes explicit licensing information under $.governance.policies. The project’s source code is governed by the GPL-2.0-or-later (GNU General Public License), which requires that derivative works and redistributed versions preserve the same copyleft terms. For governance and compliance, this means any reuse, modification, or integration of the system in other products must respect GPL obligations, including source disclosure and preservation of license notices. This policy is central to how downstream usage must be evaluated and approved.
governance-officer.changes:
- $.governance.policies now explicitly documents that the source code is licensed under GPL-2.0-or-later (GNU General Public License), clarifying legal and compliance expectations for reuse and redistribution.
- The update strengthens alignment between the documented ML System Card and the project’s LICENSE.txt file, reducing ambiguity for internal and external auditors.
- Governance stakeholders should ensure that any integrations with non-GPL-compatible components are reviewed for license compatibility and that downstream users are informed of copyleft requirements referenced in $.governance.policies.

ml-engineer.overview: This update expands the ML System Card’s developer-focused section for the Bayesian hierarchical model at scale. Core implementation remains in Python and Jupyter Notebooks as captured in $.devInsight.codeOverview.languages. The card now more explicitly documents (⚠️) the end‑to‑end data flow in $.devInsight.architecture.dataFlow, dependency stack in $.devInsight.architecture.depsSummary, public model APIs in $.devInsight.architecture.publicApis, and the main components and entrypoints in $.devInsight.codeOverview.components and $.devInsight.codeOverview.entrypoints. Testing signals are summarized in $.devInsight.qualitySignals.* (⚠️).
ml-engineer.changes:
- Added/updated architecture description fields in $.devInsight.architecture.dataFlow, $.devInsight.architecture.depsSummary, and $.devInsight.architecture.publicApis to capture data pipeline, dependencies, and callable interfaces (⚠️).
- Refined code-level mapping in $.devInsight.codeOverview.components and $.devInsight.codeOverview.entrypoints to point to preprocessing, modeling, and evaluation artifacts (⚠️).
- Confirmed primary implementation languages via $.devInsight.codeOverview.languages (Python, Jupyter Notebook).
- Documented presence of tests in $.devInsight.qualitySignals.testsPresent and tentative coverage behavior in $.devInsight.qualitySignals.coverageHint (⚠️).

product-manager.overview: This system applies a Bayesian hierarchical model to forecast daily sales across many retail stores (⚠️ $.business.useCase). It is positioned as a reproducible, educational example rather than a fully managed product (⚠️ $.business.intendedUse, $.business.nonGoals). Primary users are technical profiles such as data scientists and statisticians exploring scalable Bayesian workflows (⚠️ $.business.userPopulations). The repository emphasizes batch experimentation over real-time serving (⚠️ $.business.outOfScopeUse).
product-manager.changes:
- Clarified high-level purpose and positioning of the project as a scalable Bayesian sales-forecasting example (⚠️ $.business.executiveSummary).
- Stated the main forecasting use case more explicitly: daily retail store sales via a Bayesian hierarchical model (⚠️ $.business.useCase).
- Documented intended use and non-goals, distinguishing educational/research scenarios from production services (⚠️ $.business.intendedUse, $.business.nonGoals).
- Added warnings about hazardous use in safety-critical or regulated contexts (⚠️ $.business.hazardousUseCases).
- Described out-of-scope, such as real-time, low-latency prediction serving (⚠️ $.business.outOfScopeUse).
- Specified target user populations (⚠️ $.business.userPopulations).

project-manager.overview: The system is a scalable Bayesian hierarchical model for forecasting daily sales across many retail stores (see $.business.useCase). It is positioned ⚠️ as a reproducible, educational reference implementation rather than a production service (see $.business.intendedUse). ⚠️ Documentation highlights who is expected to use it—primarily data scientists and statisticians exploring Bayesian hierarchical modeling for large panels (see $.business.userPopulations)—and clarifies what is not in scope, such as managed APIs or real-time serving (see $.business.nonGoals, $.business.outOfScopeUse). Hazardous or safety‑critical uses are explicitly discouraged ⚠️ (see $.business.hazardousUseCases).
project-manager.changes:
- Clarified or expanded the high-level description of the project’s purpose in $.business.executiveSummary ⚠️.
- Updated the intended use and non-goals to better emphasize the educational, non-production focus in $.business.intendedUse and $.business.nonGoals ⚠️.
- Refined descriptions of out-of-scope and hazardous use cases in $.business.outOfScopeUse and $.business.hazardousUseCases ⚠️.
- Confirmed and slightly detailed the main forecasting use case for multi-store daily sales in $.business.useCase.
- Adjusted the description of target user groups in $.business.userPopulations ⚠️.

software-developer.overview: This system provides a Bayesian hierarchical model for forecasting daily sales across many retail stores ($.business.useCase). The repository focuses on scalable, batched modeling workflows implemented in Python and related tooling, targeting software practitioners who work with probabilistic forecasting and experimentation. It is primarily designed as a reproducible, educational example of how to structure and run large-scale Bayesian models in a modern ML/analytics stack ⚠️ ($.business.executiveSummary, $.business.intendedUse). Non-goals include running a production-grade, low-latency forecasting API ⚠️ ($.business.nonGoals, $.business.outOfScopeUse). Main users are expected to be data scientists and developers working with statistical modeling ⚠️ ($.business.userPopulations).
software-developer.changes:
- Clarified the core forecasting scenario as daily multi-store retail sales using a Bayesian hierarchical approach ($.business.useCase).
- Expanded the high-level description of the repository’s purpose and positioning ⚠️ ($.business.executiveSummary).
- Added/updated intended-use guidance, emphasizing educational and reproducible research usage rather than production deployment ⚠️ ($.business.intendedUse, $.business.nonGoals).
- Documented out-of-scope usage, especially real-time or safety-critical integrations ⚠️ ($.business.outOfScopeUse, $.business.hazardousUseCases).
- Refined description of target user populations (data scientists, statisticians, and developers interested in Bayesian methods) ⚠️ ($.business.userPopulations).

ux-researcher.overview: This system focuses on forecasting daily sales across many retail stores using a Bayesian hierarchical model (see $.business.useCase). It is implemented as a research/analysis codebase rather than a production service ⚠️ (see $.business.nonGoals). The primary intended use is likely educational and exploratory work around scalable Bayesian modeling and time-series forecasting ⚠️ (see $.business.intendedUse). Core users appear to be data scientists and statisticians working with hierarchical time-series data ⚠️ (see $.business.userPopulations). UX concerns will mainly arise in how experiment workflows, notebooks, and documentation support these technical users’ tasks.
ux-researcher.changes:
- Clarified or expanded the main forecasting scenario for retail sales using a Bayesian hierarchical model (see $.business.useCase).
- Added or revised a brief repository-level executive summary of goals and scope ⚠️ (see $.business.executiveSummary).
- Updated descriptions of intended educational/research use and de-emphasized production deployment ⚠️ (see $.business.intendedUse, $.business.nonGoals).
- Further specified out-of-scope and hazardous use cases, including safety-critical or regulated decisions based directly on forecasts ⚠️ (see $.business.hazardousUseCases, $.business.outOfScopeUse).
- Slightly refined the description of target user populations (technical data/ML practitioners) ⚠️ (see $.business.userPopulations).

note.overview: The system is a scalable Bayesian hierarchical model for forecasting daily sales across many retail stores (see $.business.useCase). It is positioned ⚠️ as a reproducible, educational reference implementation rather than a production service (see $.business.intendedUse). ⚠️ Documentation highlights who is expected to use it—primarily data scientists and statisticians exploring Bayesian hierarchical modeling for large panels (see $.business.userPopulations)—and clarifies what is not in scope, such as managed APIs or real-time serving (see $.business.nonGoals, $.business.outOfScopeUse). Hazardous or safety‑critical uses are explicitly discouraged ⚠️ (see $.business.hazardousUseCases).
note.changes:
- Clarified or expanded the high-level description of the project’s purpose in $.business.executiveSummary ⚠️.
- Updated the intended use and non-goals to better emphasize the educational, non-production focus in $.business.intendedUse and $.business.nonGoals ⚠️.
- Refined descriptions of out-of-scope and hazardous use cases in $.business.outOfScopeUse and $.business.hazardousUseCases ⚠️.
- Confirmed and slightly detailed the main forecasting use case for multi-store daily sales in $.business.useCase.
- Adjusted the description of target user groups in $.business.userPopulations ⚠️.

note.overview: This system provides a Bayesian hierarchical model for forecasting daily sales across many retail stores ($.business.useCase). The repository focuses on scalable, batched modeling workflows implemented in Python and related tooling, targeting software practitioners who work with probabilistic forecasting and experimentation. It is primarily designed as a reproducible, educational example of how to structure and run large-scale Bayesian models in a modern ML/analytics stack ⚠️ ($.business.executiveSummary, $.business.intendedUse). Non-goals include running a production-grade, low-latency forecasting API ⚠️ ($.business.nonGoals, $.business.outOfScopeUse). Main users are expected to be data scientists and developers working with statistical modeling ⚠️ ($.business.userPopulations).
note.changes:
- Clarified the core forecasting scenario as daily multi-store retail sales using a Bayesian hierarchical approach ($.business.useCase).
- Expanded the high-level description of the repository’s purpose and positioning ⚠️ ($.business.executiveSummary).
- Added/updated intended-use guidance, emphasizing educational and reproducible research usage rather than production deployment ⚠️ ($.business.intendedUse, $.business.nonGoals).
- Documented out-of-scope usage, especially real-time or safety-critical integrations ⚠️ ($.business.outOfScopeUse, $.business.hazardousUseCases).
- Refined description of target user populations (data scientists, statisticians, and developers interested in Bayesian methods) ⚠️ ($.business.userPopulations).

note.overview: This system focuses on forecasting daily sales across many retail stores using a Bayesian hierarchical model (see $.business.useCase). It is implemented as a research/analysis codebase rather than a production service ⚠️ (see $.business.nonGoals). The primary intended use is likely educational and exploratory work around scalable Bayesian modeling and time-series forecasting ⚠️ (see $.business.intendedUse). Core users appear to be data scientists and statisticians working with hierarchical time-series data ⚠️ (see $.business.userPopulations). UX concerns will mainly arise in how experiment workflows, notebooks, and documentation support these technical users’ tasks.
note.changes:
- Clarified or expanded the main forecasting scenario for retail sales using a Bayesian hierarchical model (see $.business.useCase).
- Added or revised a brief repository-level executive summary of goals and scope ⚠️ (see $.business.executiveSummary).
- Updated descriptions of intended educational/research use and de-emphasized production deployment ⚠️ (see $.business.intendedUse, $.business.nonGoals).
- Further specified out-of-scope and hazardous use cases, including safety-critical or regulated decisions based directly on forecasts ⚠️ (see $.business.hazardousUseCases, $.business.outOfScopeUse).
- Slightly refined the description of target user populations (technical data/ML practitioners) ⚠️ (see $.business.userPopulations).
